worth_it_explanation: The campaign and ad group structure is the skeleton that every other Kokai optimization decision builds on. Koa, The Trade Desk's AI optimization layer, learns from conversion signal at the ad group level, so an account where signal is thinly spread across many small ad groups starves the algorithm of the data it needs to bid intelligently. These checks are configuration reviews inside the Kokai platform that take under an hour and surface problems that compound over the life of the campaign. A restructure before a flight locks in is far less disruptive than one mid-flight.
intro: The Trade Desk's Kokai platform organizes buying through a three-level hierarchy: Advertiser, Campaign, and Ad Group. Campaigns hold the flight dates, budget, and goal. Ad groups hold the targeting, bid strategy, and creatives that Koa uses to optimize toward the campaign goal. These checks confirm that the hierarchy is coherent and goal-aligned, that ad groups are sized to accumulate the conversion signal Koa needs, and that the key performance indicator (KPI) is set at the level where optimization actually runs.

audit_checks.title: Campaign and ad group hierarchy does not reflect distinct goals or audiences
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When campaigns and ad groups are organized by convenience rather than by goal or audience, Koa optimizes across targeting that should never compete for the same conversion signal. Reporting becomes unreadable, budget does not flow to the highest-performing segments, and structural fixes later in the flight require pausing and rebuilding.

educate.base:
In The Trade Desk's Kokai platform, the Campaign level sets the budget, flight dates, and the goal that Koa optimizes toward. The Ad Group level holds the targeting parameters, bid strategy, and creatives. The two levels have different jobs, and the hierarchy works best when it reflects a real organizing principle: separate campaigns for separate business goals or funnel stages, and separate ad groups for separate audiences or formats within a goal.

An account that treats campaigns and ad groups as interchangeable containers ends up with Koa making optimization decisions across targeting combinations that should not share a budget or a conversion goal. A prospecting ad group and a retargeting ad group sitting inside the same campaign compete for the same budget, even though the audiences and the expected conversion rates are different. A single campaign mixing video and display ad groups makes frequency and viewability reporting impossible to interpret by channel.

A coherent hierarchy also matters for Kokai's pacing and forecasting tools. Campaigns with a clear goal let the platform model spend and reach accurately against a defined objective. Campaigns that hold a mix of unrelated ad groups produce pacing signals that do not correspond to any single goal, so the platform's budget delivery becomes a guess.

A few signs point to a misaligned hierarchy:
  • A campaign mixes prospecting and retargeting ad groups without separate budget controls or goals
  • Ad groups in the same campaign target overlapping audiences with different expected conversion rates
  • Campaign names reference generic labels like 'Q2 test' or an internal ticket number rather than a goal or segment
  • Campaigns hold a single ad group, suggesting the campaign level serves no structural purpose and ad groups were added without a plan
You may want to map each campaign to a single business goal before auditing the ad groups inside it. If a campaign cannot be summarized in one sentence that names a goal and an audience type, it may be doing too much.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an e-commerce advertiser, a common and workable structure separates prospecting campaigns targeting new audiences from retargeting campaigns targeting site visitors who did not purchase. Within each, ad groups segment by product category, audience signal source (e.g., first-party list versus Data Marketplace segment), or creative format. You may want to confirm that prospecting and retargeting are never co-mingled inside a single campaign. Their conversion rates and optimal bids differ enough that shared budgets consistently disadvantage one or the other.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, campaigns organized by funnel stage (awareness, consideration, conversion) give Koa distinct goals to optimize against at each stage. Ad groups within a stage then segment by audience type or geography. You may want to confirm that awareness and conversion-oriented ad groups do not share a campaign. Awareness buys optimize toward reach and frequency while conversion buys optimize toward cost per lead, and the two pull budget in opposite directions.

learn_more.source_title: OpenRTB specification — campaign and creative structure reference

investigate.intro: Review the campaign list and map each campaign to a single goal, then confirm that the ad groups inside each campaign share that goal and do not mix audience types that should be separated.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the advertiser in Kokai and navigate to the Campaigns view. Read each campaign name and note the stated or implied goal. A campaign that cannot be summarized as a single goal plus a broad audience type warrants closer review.

Select a campaign and open its Ad Groups tab. For each ad group, note the targeting strategy: prospecting versus retargeting, first-party data versus third-party Data Marketplace segments, video versus display. Flag any ad group whose targeting strategy differs materially from the others in the same campaign.
Check whether any campaign contains a single ad group. A one-ad-group campaign offers no structural advantage over the ad group alone and suggests the hierarchy was not planned deliberately.

Review the budget allocation across campaigns. Campaigns mixing very different audience types (e.g., cold prospecting and one-to-one retargeting) inside a shared budget are a flag for goal misalignment.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the intended goal and the audience types present in its ad groups. A campaign maps to a single goal with ad groups that share a consistent targeting strategy. A campaign that mixes prospecting and retargeting, or mixes optimization goals (reach versus cost-per-acquisition), or holds a single ad group with no clear organizing principle is recorded as misaligned with the campaign name and the specific structural problem noted.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the campaign name, its intended goal, and any ad groups whose targeting strategy diverges from that goal to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the campaign and ad group hierarchy in {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk account does not consistently reflect distinct goals or audience types. Campaigns that mix prospecting and retargeting ad groups, or that combine targeting strategies with different expected conversion rates, prevent Koa from optimizing budget allocation accurately and make performance reporting difficult to interpret by segment. {{voice_recommend}} reorganizing the campaign structure so each campaign maps to a single goal and flight type, with ad groups inside each campaign targeting audiences that share the same conversion objective. This reorganization is most effective before a new flight launches. Restructuring mid-flight resets the optimization signal Koa has accumulated.

audit_checks.title: Ad groups are split so thin that none accumulates enough conversion signal for Koa to optimize
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Koa learns from conversions, not from impressions. An account with many small ad groups each holding a sliver of budget may deliver spend, but none of the ad groups sees enough conversions for the algorithm to identify which targeting parameters are working. The result is effectively unguided bidding for the entire flight.

educate.base:
Koa, The Trade Desk's AI optimization system, adjusts bids at the ad group level based on the conversion signals it observes. The algorithm needs a minimum volume of conversion events over a window of time to distinguish signal from noise and to surface the audience and contextual patterns that predict conversions. An ad group that receives a very small budget share, targets a narrow audience, or runs briefly may deliver some impressions without ever providing Koa the data it needs to move beyond a default bidding state.

This problem appears most often when accounts are structured by convention rather than by statistical logic. A practitioner splits ad groups by geography, by a data segment, by device type, and by dayparting, and the resulting matrix of twelve ad groups each receives one-twelfth of a budget that would have been enough to optimize two or three. Each ad group looks active in the platform because it is spending, but Koa is effectively guessing on all twelve because none has seen enough conversions to learn from.

The Trade Desk does not publish a single required minimum conversion count, but the dynamics are the same as those documented across programmatic platforms: an ad group needs a consistent flow of conversion events, not a handful, before an automated system can distinguish signal from variance. Ad groups with very low weekly conversion counts are candidates for consolidation.

A few signs point to fragmentation that may be starving Koa:
  • An advertiser has more than a dozen active ad groups but a flight budget that would fund only two or three at meaningful scale
  • Individual ad groups show impressions and clicks but zero or near-zero conversions over multiple weeks of a live flight
  • Ad groups segment on a dimension (e.g., device type) where The Trade Desk can handle that variation algorithmically without a structural split
  • The account has grown through additions without retirements, so old ad groups remain active alongside newer ones
You may want to compare the number of active ad groups against the weekly conversion volume before deciding that the account is at an appropriate scale. Consolidating targeting into fewer, larger ad groups and letting Koa handle within-group variation is a reasonable starting point for accounts where conversions are sparse.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an e-commerce advertiser where purchase conversions are the primary signal, you may want to confirm that no ad group is segmented so narrowly that it targets a product sub-category generating fewer than a handful of purchases per week. A product-level split that appears logical for a catalog of thousands may fragment conversion signal badly when any individual product category converts infrequently.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, form submissions are the primary conversion signal, and their volume is typically lower than e-commerce transactions. You may want to consolidate geographic ad groups into broader regional buckets rather than individual markets, particularly in campaigns where the total weekly lead count across all ad groups is low. Broader targeting gives Koa more surface area to find converting users.

learn_more.source_title: Kokai platform — ad group optimization overview

investigate.intro: Review the number of active ad groups against the flight budget and the weekly conversion volume to assess whether any ad groups are too small to accumulate signal for Koa.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Navigate to the advertiser's Campaigns view and select a campaign. Open the Ad Groups tab and note the total number of active ad groups.

Check the budget allocation across ad groups. Divide the campaign's total budget by the number of active ad groups to see the average budget per ad group. A very low average for a flight targeting a conversion goal is a flag for fragmentation.

Pull the ad group performance report for the current or most recent flight. Filter to active ad groups and sort by conversions. Note how many ad groups have recorded zero or near-zero conversions over the full flight window despite spending. These are candidates for consolidation.

Review the dimensions on which ad groups are split. If the split is by a dimension that The Trade Desk handles algorithmically, such as device type or time of day, note it as a potential consolidation opportunity.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the number of active ad groups, the total flight budget, and the weekly conversion count across those ad groups. A campaign with a small number of ad groups each receiving a budget share consistent with the total conversion volume passes. A campaign where a material share of active ad groups show spending but zero or near-zero conversions over multiple weeks is flagged, with the number of affected ad groups and the conversion counts noted.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the number of active ad groups, the budget allocation per ad group, and the conversion count per ad group over the current flight window to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk campaigns are split across more ad groups than the flight budget and conversion volume can support. Several active ad groups are delivering spend but recording zero or near-zero conversions over their flight windows, which means Koa is operating without the signal it needs to distinguish effective targeting from ineffective. The result is bidding that is close to undifferentiated across all ad groups for the duration of the flight. {{voice_recommend}} consolidating the most thinly split ad groups, prioritizing consolidation along dimensions that Koa can optimize algorithmically, such as device type or time of day, and running fewer, larger ad groups that can each accumulate meaningful conversion volume. Restructuring before the next flight launches avoids resetting accumulated optimization signal mid-flight.

audit_checks.title: The KPI or optimization goal is set at a level that does not match where Koa runs its optimization
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Koa optimizes at the ad group level toward the goal defined at the campaign level. When the goal in the platform does not match the advertiser's actual business objective, or when different ad groups inside a campaign are intended to drive different outcomes, Koa optimizes toward the wrong target for some or all of the flight budget.

educate.base:
In Kokai, the optimization goal is set at the campaign level, and Koa uses that goal to adjust bids across the ad groups in the campaign. The goal options include reach and frequency targets, click-through rate (CTR), viewability, and cost-per-action (CPA) targets tied to conversion events tracked via the Universal Pixel or a server-to-server Events integration.

A mismatch between the goal in the platform and the advertiser's actual business objective produces optimization that works as designed but toward the wrong outcome. A campaign with a reach goal where the advertiser actually wants CPA performance will deliver broad reach efficiently but will not prioritize audiences that convert. A campaign with a CTR goal where the advertiser wants view-through credit will prioritize ad formats that generate clicks, not those that generate the brand exposure the attribution model is measuring.

A subtler version of this problem appears when ad groups inside a campaign are built to serve different purposes. If one ad group runs a prospecting strategy and another runs retargeting, and the campaign goal is a single CPA target, Koa will optimize both ad groups toward that CPA target even though the expected conversion rate for cold prospecting is materially lower than for retargeting. The prospecting ad group either underspends or never reaches the CPA target, and the retargeting ad group may absorb budget that was intended to fund prospecting.

A few signs point to a goal mismatch:
  • The campaign goal in Kokai is 'Reach' or 'CTR' while the advertiser is reporting success against CPA
  • A campaign mixes prospecting and retargeting ad groups under a single CPA target
  • The Universal Pixel conversion event tied to the campaign goal tracks a micro-conversion (e.g., a page view) rather than the business outcome the advertiser cares about (e.g., a purchase or a submitted form)
  • The campaign goal references a conversion event that the Universal Pixel has not recorded any activity against in the current flight
You may want to confirm that the conversion event attached to the campaign goal is firing and that it represents the outcome the advertiser actually wants to optimize toward. A goal tied to a conversion event with zero recorded activity is the equivalent of having no goal at all.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an e-commerce advertiser, the campaign goal should reference a purchase or revenue-based conversion event, not a page view or an add-to-cart event, unless the flight is explicitly an upper-funnel awareness buy. You may want to confirm that the conversion event tied to the goal is the purchase event from the Universal Pixel or the server-to-server Events integration, and that it is recording activity in the current flight.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, the campaign goal should reference the form submission or qualified lead event, not a page view or a click event. You may want to confirm that the conversion event captures the actual submission, not the thank-you page visit. The two differ when form abandonment is high.

educate.learn_more_note: The Trade Desk maintains its documentation for this topic in its partner portal, which requires a logged-in seat and is not publicly accessible. No public reference is linked here. Practitioners with Trade Desk access can find the relevant guidance in the in-platform Help Center.

investigate.intro: Confirm that the campaign goal in Kokai matches the advertiser's actual business objective, that the conversion event attached to the goal is recording activity, and that the ad groups inside the campaign are all intended to optimize toward that same goal.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open a campaign in Kokai and review the goal setting. Note whether the goal type (reach, CTR, viewability, CPA) matches what the advertiser has described as their objective for this flight.

If the goal is CPA, note which conversion event it references. Navigate to the Universal Pixel or Events configuration and confirm the event has recorded conversions during the current flight window. A CPA goal with zero conversions means Koa has no signal to optimize against.

Review the ad groups inside the campaign. For each, note the targeting strategy: prospecting versus retargeting, brand versus non-brand audience. If ad groups with materially different expected conversion rates share a single CPA goal, flag the campaign for goal or structural separation.

Check whether the conversion event attached to the goal is the right business event. A goal tied to a micro-conversion (e.g., a product page view) when the advertiser's KPI is purchases means Koa optimizes toward the wrong outcome.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the goal type in Kokai, the conversion event it references (if CPA), the event's conversion count during the current flight, and whether the ad groups inside the campaign are all designed to optimize toward that goal. A campaign with a goal that matches the advertiser's business objective, a conversion event that is recording activity, and ad groups that share a consistent optimization target passes. A campaign whose goal mismatches the advertiser's KPI, whose conversion event has zero activity, or whose ad groups mix prospecting and retargeting under a single CPA target is recorded as failing with the specific mismatch noted.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the campaign goal, the conversion event it references, the event's conversion count for the current flight, and any ad groups whose optimization target diverges from the campaign goal to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more campaigns in {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk account have a goal setting that does not match the advertiser's actual business objective, or have a conversion event attached to the goal that has not recorded activity during the flight. Koa optimizes at the ad group level toward the goal defined at the campaign level, so a mismatched or inactive goal means the platform bids without a signal that reflects the desired outcome. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the goal setting on each active campaign, updating the conversion event to one that reflects the primary business objective and has confirmed activity, and separating campaigns whose ad groups are intended to optimize toward materially different outcomes. Where a campaign mixes prospecting and retargeting under a single conversion-per-acquisition goal, {{voice_recommend}} splitting those into separate campaigns with goals calibrated to each audience's expected conversion rate.
